steps.title: Step 1
steps.content: Identify the sampling method. Viewers of a streaming news program voluntarily visited the program's website and chose to respond. This is a voluntary response (self-selected) sample, not a random sample.

steps.title: Step 2
steps.content: For a poll to represent the US population, respondents must be randomly selected. Here, only motivated program viewers responded — they share demographics and viewpoints not representative of the broader public.

steps.title: Step 3
steps.content:
Evaluate each choice:
  • Choice A — The percentages do not add up to 100%, so any possible conclusions from the poll are invalid.: The percentages sum to 98%, meaning 2% of respondents may not have selected either listed response. A gap this small does not invalidate conclusions; representativeness is the real issue.
  • Choice B — Those who responded to the poll were not a random sample of the population of the United States.: Respondents self-selected from the program's audience; they were not a random sample of the US population. This directly explains why results are unlikely to represent national sentiments. That is the correct answer.
  • Choice C — There were not 50% "Yes" responses and 50% "No" responses.: There is no rule requiring a valid poll to show a 50/50 split. An unequal split is perfectly valid in a properly conducted random sample.
  • Choice D — The program did not allow viewers enough time to respond to the poll.: The amount of time given to respond does not address the core problem of non-random, self-selected participation.
